1: The vision of the throne-chariot revisited — the expanse above the heads of the cherubim (Ezekiel 1:22-26 vocabulary) reappears here. The comparison to sapphire (keʾeven sappir) is the same descriptor used in Ezekiel 1:26. Chapter 10 is a reprise of the chapter 1 vision but now contextualized within the Jerusalem temple rather than the Babylonian plain: the throne-chariot is inside the temple courts, preparing to depart. The throne-chariot's presence in the temple reflects the OT tradition of the divine warrior's chariot-throne (Psalms 68:17-18) entering the sanctuary.
2: The man in linen commanded to take coals from between the cherubim and scatter them over the city — the coal-taking is the act of judgment that corresponds to the purification in Isaiah 6:6 (the coal from the altar purifies the prophet's lips) but here the coals scattered over Jerusalem indicate burning judgment rather than cleansing. The Babylonian burning of Jerusalem (2 Kings 25:9) is the historical event this action foreshadows: the city was literally burned when it fell in 586 BCE.
3: The position of the cherubim — on the south side of the temple when the man entered — is the side of the altar of burnt offerings (the outer altar stood in the south portion of the inner court). The cloud filling the inner court when the glory moved from the cherub to the temple threshold (v4) is the standard theophanic cloud of divine presence (Exodus 40:34-35; 1 Kings 8:10-11). The filling of the house with the cloud and the court with the brightness of divine glory echoes the Solomonic dedication-narrative, but here it is the prelude to departure rather than arrival.
4: The glory of YHWH rising from the cherub to the threshold — the first step of the departure sequence. The cloud and brightness filling the temple is a transitional state: the glory is not yet departed but is in motion, lifting off from the cherub-throne to the threshold. The threshold is the liminal space between the temple's interior holiness and the court — a theologically significant intermediate position.
5: The sound of the wings of the cherubim heard in the outer court like the voice of God Almighty (El Shaddai) — the divine name El Shaddai appears only here in Ezekiel. The name is associated with the patriarchal period and with divine power (Genesis 17:1; Ezekiel 28:3; Ezekiel 35:11; Exodus 6:3). Its use here marks the sound of the departing glory as the unmistakable voice of the divine power-name — the cherubim's wings create a sound equivalent to the divine speech.
6: The command given to the man in linen to take fire from between the wheels, among the cherubim — the connection between the wheels (ofanim) and the cherubim in Ezekiel 10 is closer than in Ezekiel 1. The wheels were between the cherubim (not beside them as in Ezekiel 1), and the fire that had to be collected from them is the altar-fire that burns between the living creatures in Ezekiel 1:13. The altar-fire as judgment-fire is the theological point: what purifies in the cult becomes the instrument of judgment on the city.
7: One cherub stretching out its hand from between the cherubim and putting fire into the hands of the man in linen — the cherub's active participation in the coal-transfer is unique to Ezekiel 10. In Ezekiel 1, the living creatures were entirely under divine direction; here one cherub actively assists the judgment-coal transfer. The angelic cooperation in the divine judgment is the throne-chariot vision's contribution to Ezekiel's theology of cosmic judgment.
8: The form of a human hand (tavnit yad ʾadam) beneath the wings of the cherubim — this detail adds to the Ezekiel 1 description (where only the feet of the living creatures were described). The human hand beneath the wings of the angelic figures has been interpreted as: (1) a hand to grasp or carry objects (as in v7); (2) a symbol of the humanoid character of the divine chariot's attendants; (3) an architectural reference to the hand-shaped brackets visible in Phoenician and Canaanite ivory carvings of divine attendants.
9: The four wheels beside each cherub — "the appearance of the wheels was like chrysolite" (keʿein tarshish) — the gem identification follows Ezekiel 1:16 precisely. The mineral tarshish (translated variously as topaz, chrysolite, or beryl) was a Mediterranean luxury import whose shimmer suggested the metallic fire-color of the visionary wheels. The ANE throne-chariot was a functional vehicle drawn by divine animals (the divine horses of 2 Kgs 6:17 and Psalms 68:17); Ezekiel's chariot has wheels with their own living spirit (ruach ḥayyah) inside them.
10: The four wheels the same appearance, each as if a wheel within a wheel — the "wheel within a wheel" (ofan betoikh ofan) description has fascinated interpreters and illustrators for centuries. The most likely ANE referent is the double-wheel chariot used in Mesopotamian military vehicles (visible on Assyrian reliefs): an outer wheel with wide spokes and an inner solid disk creating a compound wheel structure. The visionary adaptation makes the wheels able to move in all four directions without turning.
11: The directional movement without turning — the same feature as in Ezekiel 1:12, 17: "they went in any of the four directions without turning as they went." This technical description of the wheels' omnidirectional capacity without rotation is the most machine-like element of the entire vision. It distinguishes the divine chariot from all human vehicles and has attracted engineering interpretation (Jakob Lenz, 1696 and many later) as well as mystical interpretation (the Merkabah tradition).
12: The bodies, backs, hands, and wings of the cherubim — and the wheels — full of eyes all around: the eyes covering every surface of the chariot-living-creatures is the omniscience motif made visual. Psalms 139:7-12 ("whither shall I go from your Spirit?") is the theological equivalent: YHWH's awareness is omnidirectional and total. In Mesopotamian iconography, divine figures are sometimes depicted with multiple eyes or with a star-pattern that can suggest omniscient vision; Ezekiel's eye-covered wheels make the divine omniscience structurally intrinsic to the chariot itself.
13: "As for the wheels, they were called in my hearing: the whirling wheels" (hagalgal) — the naming of the wheels ("the Whirler" or "the Roller") by an audible divine declaration within the vision is unique in Ezekiel. The term galgal (wheel/rolling thing) is used in Psalms 77:18 and 83:14 for the rolling thunder or tumbleweeds of the storm-theophany; here it becomes the proper name for the chariot-wheels.
14: The four faces of the cherubim: first face is a cherub-face, second a human face, third a lion, fourth an eagle — here the ox-face of Ezekiel 1 has become a cherub-face. The substitution of "cherub" for "ox" in the first face-position may explain why the ox imagery in the Jerusalem temple (the twelve oxen supporting the bronze sea, 1 Kings 7:25) was a source of contamination — associating cherubim with cattle in the popular mind led to the golden calf worship at Bethel (1 Kings 12:28-29). The cherub-face replacing the ox-face corrects this association.
15: The identification of the living creatures from Ezekiel 1 as cherubim — made explicit in v20 as well — grounds the visionary beings in the established OT iconography of the ark-cherubim (Exodus 25:18-22; 1 Kings 6:23-28). The cherubim of the Most Holy Place were winged figures flanking the mercy-seat above the ark; the cherubim of Ezekiel's vision are their cosmic counterparts, the living creatures that bear the divine throne across the heavens and through history.
16: The wheels rising when the cherubim rise — the unity of living creatures and wheels is the distinctive feature of the chariot vision. In ANE military chariots, the horses or oxen and the chariot were distinct; Ezekiel's vision integrates the motive power (the living creatures) and the vehicle (the wheels) into a single organism. The theological implication: YHWH's mobility across creation is not mediated through separate components but is intrinsic to a unified divine presence.
17: The spirit of the living creatures in the wheels — the same as Ezekiel 1:21. The ruach ḥayyah (spirit of the living creature) animating the wheels directly, with no mechanical intermediary, is the vision's answer to the question of what makes the divine chariot move. The answer is not mechanical or cosmological but personal: the living spirit that is in the living creatures is also in the wheels, giving them their omnidirectional mobility.
18: The glory of YHWH departing from the threshold of the house and standing over the cherubim — the second step of the departure sequence. The glory has moved from (1) the cherub-throne in the Most Holy Place to (2) the temple threshold (v4) to (3) above the cherubim in the inner court. The graduated withdrawal from the temple is the theological preparation for the final departure in Ezekiel 11:22-23 to the Mount of Olives and then to Babylon (implied in the deportation-context).
19: The cherubim lifting their wings and rising from the earth before Ezekiel's eyes — the departure from the Jerusalem temple is a historically unprecedented event in Israelite theology. The temple had been the permanent dwelling of YHWH ("a place for you to dwell forever," 1 Kings 8:13); its abandonment by the divine glory was theologically catastrophic. The departure moves to the east gate — the same gate through which the glory will return in Ezekiel 43:1-4, completing the structural arc of the book's theology.
20: The explicit identification of the living creatures from Ezekiel 1 as the cherubim beneath the God of Israel — Ezekiel confirms that the Babylonian vision and the Jerusalem temple vision are of the same divine realities. The cherubim of the heavenly throne-chariot are the cosmic counterparts of the golden cherubim in the Most Holy Place. The vision asserts the continuity of YHWH's presence across the radical geographical disruption of the exile: the God who was in the Jerusalem temple is the same God now present at the Chebar canal in Babylon.
21: The four faces and four wings of each cherub, with human hands beneath their wings — the cross-reference to Ezekiel 1 is the literary device that confirms the identity of the two vision-experiences. Ezekiel's vision at the Chebar (Ezekiel 1:1) and the vision in Jerusalem (Ezekiel 8:3) are connected by these repeated details: YHWH's presence is not geographically restricted despite the exile's apparent evidence to the contrary.
22: The faces identical in appearance to the living creatures of Ezekiel 1 — the precision of the identification (same appearance, same forward movement) confirms that the throne-chariot is a consistent reality, not a shifting vision. The theological claim of Ezekiel 1-11 taken together: YHWH's glory was in Jerusalem, witnessed and cataloged its abominations, departed in stages through the east gate, and now accompanies the exiles in Babylon. The exile is not divine abandonment — it is divine accompaniment. The prophet who saw the glory at the Chebar (Ezekiel 1) also sees it depart Jerusalem (Ezekiel 10-11), confirming that YHWH himself is in exile with his people.
